display_name: Error rates matter more than absolute counter values
context: Evaluating interface error counters from show interface or similar output
observation: Large absolute error counter values on an interface
naive_interpretation: A high absolute number of errors means the interface is in bad shape
correct_interpretation: The error rate (errors per second or errors relative to total packets) is what matters, not the absolute count. An interface passing 10 billion packets with 1000 errors over 30 days has a 0.00001% error rate — essentially clean. The same 1000 errors in 5 minutes on a lightly loaded link is a serious problem.
why: Absolute counters grow indefinitely with uptime — a 6-month-old counter set will always look large
Error significance is relative to traffic volume and time window
Industry standard for acceptable bit error rate on Ethernet is 10^-12 or better

confirmation_commands.purpose: Get total packet counts to calculate error rate as a percentage of total traffic
Determine how long counters have been accumulating to calculate errors per unit time

confirmation_commands.command: show interfaces GigabitEthernet1/0/1 | include packets input|packets output|errors
confirmation_commands.purpose: Get total packets and errors for rate calculation

traps: Panicking over a counter showing 50000 errors without checking that the interface has passed 500 billion packets
Comparing absolute counters across interfaces with different traffic loads or uptimes
Not normalizing error counts by time period to get an error rate
